elements.sent-2411-001: Nearest neighbor (NN) algorithms have been extensively used for missing data problems in recommender systems and sequential decision-making systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Prior theoretical analysis has established favorable guarantees for NN when the underlying data is sufficiently smooth and the missingness probabilities are lower bounded.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here we analyze NN with non-smooth non-linear functions with vast amounts of missingness.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, we consider matrix completion settings where the entries of the underlying matrix follow a latent non-linear factor model, with the non-linearity belonging to a \Holder function class that is less smooth than Lipschitz.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our results establish following favorable properties for a suitable two-sided NN: (1) The mean squared error (MSE) of NN adapts to the smoothness of the non-linearity, (2) under certain regularity conditions, the NN error rate matches the rate obtained by an oracle equipped with the knowledge of both the row and column latent factors, and finally (3) NN's MSE is non-trivial for a wide range of settings even when several matrix entries might be missing deterministically.
elements.sent-2411-006: We support our theoretical findings via extensive numerical simulations and a case study with data from a mobile health study, HeartSteps.
elements.sent-2411-007: Latent factor models are ubiquitous in recommendation systems, panel data settings, sequential decision-making problems, and in various other scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-008: Matrix completion is a crucial problem in this context.

elements.paragraph-2411-012: Runtime Complexity of NN based methods
elements.sent-2411-402: In terms of runtime complexity for a $n \times m$ matrix, Row - NN has $\mathcal{O ({n\choose2 m + n) = \mathcal{O (n^2m +n)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-403: First term arises as there are ${n\choose 2}$ combinations of rows and computing $L_2$ distance between each pair of rows take $\mathcal{O (m)$ time.}
elements.sent-2411-404: The second term arises due to aggregation of $\mathcal{O (n)$ terms.}
elements.sent-2411-405: Similarly Col - NN has a runtime complexity of $\mathcal{O (m^2n +m)$.}
elements.sent-2411-406: Finally, TS-NN has a runtime complexity of $\mathcal{O (m^2n +n^2m + mn)$.}
elements.sent-2411-407: DR-NN can be expressed as a linear combination of Row-NN, Col-NN and TS-NN~\citep{dwivedi2022doubly , hence it also has a runtime complexity of $\mathcal{O (m^2n +n^2m + mn)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-408: Just for clarity, the Row-NN algorithm is outlined below:.
elements.sent-2411-409: Compute the pairwise row distance estimates $\widehat d^2_{row (i , j)$ for all $i, j \in [n]$ and use it to construct the neighborhood of row $i$, \begin{equation}\cn_{row}(i) =\{j \in [n]: \hat{d}_{row}^2(i, j )\leq \eta_{row}\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-411: The estimate of $\theta_{i,t}$ is given by the row nearest neighbor estimate, \begin{equation}\widehat \theta_{i, t} = \frac{\sum_{j \in \cn_{row}(i)}X_{j,t}A_{j, t}}{|\cn_{row}(i)|},\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-412: Col-NN has the column-counterpart algorithm of the above procedure.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Deffered details of real-life case study: HeartSteps
elements.sent-2411-413: We will now provide additional details about the HeartSteps dataset.
elements.sent-2411-414: We observe that at each decision time point, the HeartSteps algorithm determined whether a user is available based on certain attributes like whether the user was driving a car, etc. Other features are whether the user had an active connection at or around the decision time, was not in transit and phone was not in snooze mode.
elements.sent-2411-415: We focus on the matrix completion at the ``available decision times".
elements.sent-2411-416: After screening out the ``non-avaliable times", we see that only few users have $>210$ decision times, resulting in filtering out the remaining columns/ decision times from consideration.
elements.sent-2411-417: Ultimately, we work with a dataset of 37 rows/users and 210 columns/decision times.
elements.sent-2411-418: For further details, we refer the readers to \cite{klasnja2019efficacy .}
elements.sent-2411-419: For implementation, we use 5-fold blocked cross validation.
elements.sent-2411-420: HeartSteps' underlying recommender algorithm uses a user's history to sequentially assign interventions.
elements.sent-2411-421: So, there is a temporal dependence of the later columns on the previous columns.
elements.sent-2411-422: To tackle that, we first of all divide the the rows of the matrix into 5 folds.
elements.sent-2411-423: Now in each iteration we fix a fold, we hold out the entries in the last 40 columns of those rows as our test dataset.
elements.sent-2411-424: Remaining entries are used for training the NNs, USVT and SoftImpute.
elements.sent-2411-425: For NNs, we use the tuned $\eta_{row}$ and $\eta_{col}$(or one of them for Row-NN and Col-NN) to complete the matrix.
elements.sent-2411-426: Then we report the difference between hold-out entries and their corresponding fitted estimates.
elements.sent-2411-427: We see the estimated matrices in SoftImpute and USVT resulted in extremely high test errors compared to NN-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-428: For $\eta_{grid,row ,\eta_{grid,col}$ in one-sided NNs and DR-NN, we work with the percentiles 25-85.}
elements.sent-2411-429: Low percentiles were avoided as they gave the lowest training errors but the test errors were exorbitantly high.
elements.sent-2411-430: For TS-NN $\mbi{\eta}$ grid, we consider percentiles 8 - 50.
